questions.question: What is the approximate capacity of short-term (working) memory?
questions.explanation: George Miller's (1956) classic finding: working memory holds approximately 7 ± 2 items (chunks). This has been refined to 4 ± 1 by modern research (Cowan, 2001).

questions.question: Multitasking with complex tasks is as efficient as doing them sequentially.
questions.explanation: Research consistently shows that 'multitasking' with complex tasks is actually rapid task-switching, which incurs a cognitive cost (10-40% time penalty). True parallel processing only works for highly automatic tasks.

questions.question: Match each cognitive bias to its description:

right: Seeking info that confirms existing beliefs
Over-relying on first piece of information
Judging likelihood by ease of recall

questions.explanation: These biases represent systematic errors in thinking: confirmation bias (selective evidence), anchoring (first number sticks), Dunning-Kruger (incompetent don't know they're incompetent), availability (vivid = common).

questions.question: The ___ effect describes how the way information is presented (gain vs. loss framing) changes decisions.
questions.explanation: The framing effect (Tversky & Kahneman, 1981): people choose differently when the same outcome is framed as a gain ('90% survival rate') vs. a loss ('10% mortality rate'). Same data, different decisions.

questions.question: According to Dual Process Theory, System 1 thinking is characterized by being:
questions.explanation: Kahneman's Dual Process Theory: System 1 is fast, automatic, intuitive (driving, face recognition). System 2 is slow, effortful, deliberate (complex math, logical reasoning). Most daily decisions use System 1.

questions.question: A UX designer wants to increase organ donor registration. Currently 15% opt-in rate. Which cognitive principle would be most effective?

scenario.context: Government website for driver's license renewal. Users must actively check a box to become organ donors.

options: Make the form more visually appealing with emotional imagery
Switch from opt-in to opt-out default (pre-checked box)
Add social proof ('80% of your neighbors are donors')
Provide more educational information about donation
questions.explanation: Default effect: people overwhelmingly stick with the pre-selected option (status quo bias). Countries that switched from opt-in to opt-out saw donation rates jump from ~15% to 85-99%. The default is the most powerful nudge.

questions.question: Arrange Maslow's hierarchy of needs from base (most fundamental) to peak:
questions.explanation: Maslow's hierarchy (1943): Physiological (food, water) → Safety (security, stability) → Love/Belonging (relationships) → Esteem (respect, achievement) → Self-actualization (reaching potential).

questions.question: The 'cocktail party effect' demonstrates our ability to:
options: Selectively attend to one voice among many
questions.explanation: The cocktail party effect (Cherry, 1953) shows selective attention: we can focus on one conversation in a noisy room while filtering others. We also detect our name in unattended channels (breakthrough).

questions.question: A student studies for 6 hours straight but performs poorly on the exam. Diagnose the cognitive issue:

presentation.context: University student, re-reads textbook chapters repeatedly for 6 hours before exam.
findings: Study method: passive re-reading of highlighted text
Exam requires applying concepts to novel problems

options: Illusion of fluency — re-reading creates false sense of knowing without deep processing
Insufficient study time — 6 hours isn't enough
questions.explanation: Illusion of fluency: passive re-reading makes material feel familiar (recognition) without building retrieval strength (recall). Active recall, spaced repetition, and practice testing are far more effective despite feeling harder.

questions.question: If the forgetting curve shows 50% retention after 1 day without review, and each review doubles the retention interval, after how many reviews will the material last 16 days?
questions.explanation: Starting interval: 1 day. After 1 review: 2 days. After 2: 4 days. After 3: 8 days. After 4: 16 days. This is the principle behind spaced repetition systems (Anki, SuperMemo).

questions.question: The 'planning fallacy' causes people to consistently overestimate how long tasks will take.
questions.explanation: The planning fallacy (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979) causes people to UNDERESTIMATE time, costs, and risks while OVERESTIMATING benefits. We're optimistic planners — projects almost always take longer than predicted.

questions.question: Should social media platforms use cognitive biases (variable rewards, social proof) to maximize engagement?
questions.scenario: Platforms like Instagram use intermittent reinforcement (variable reward schedule), social validation (likes), and loss aversion (streaks) — the same mechanisms as slot machines — to keep users scrolling.

options.label: Yes — users choose to engage; it's entertainment like any other
No — exploiting cognitive vulnerabilities is inherently manipulative
options.framework: Kantian — treat users as ends, not means

options.label: Regulate with mandatory 'time spent' notifications and opt-out defaults
Require ethical review boards for engagement features, like medical IRBs

questions.explanation: Ethics review boards would apply existing bioethics principles to technology. When a design deliberately exploits cognitive vulnerabilities at scale, it parallels research ethics concerns — informed consent, harm prevention, and beneficence.

questions.question: A psychology experiment shows participants 20 words. After a distraction task, they recall words. Which positions have highest recall?
questions.code:
# Serial Position Effect
positions = list(range(1, 21))
# Primacy: first ~4 words remembered well
# Recency: last ~4 words remembered well
# Middle: poorest recall
# What shape does the recall curve form?
questions.explanation: The serial position effect produces a U-shaped recall curve: high recall for first items (primacy effect — rehearsed into LTM) and last items (recency effect — still in STM), with a dip in the middle.

questions.question: A researcher claims to have 'proven' that listening to Mozart makes you smarter. Find the flaw in their experimental design:
questions.code:
Experiment:
- Group A: Listen to Mozart for 10 min, then take IQ test
- Group B: Sit in silence for 10 min, then take IQ test
- Result: Group A scores 5 points higher
- Conclusion: 'Mozart music increases intelligence'
options: Confound: arousal/mood — any stimulating activity would boost performance vs. boredom
Sample size isn't mentioned — could be too small for significance
IQ tests can't be affected by 10-minute interventions
The researcher should have used a double-blind design
questions.explanation: The 'Mozart Effect' confounds music with arousal. Controls should include other music, podcasts, or any engaging activity. The effect disappeared when studies used proper active controls — it's arousal, not Mozart specifically.
